CHAPTER 14
CONTROL ON IMMIGRATION FROM THE COMMONWEALTH

No exact record of immigrants was maintained before control was introduced, but a rough calculation of persons from Commonwealth countries in Asia, East and West Africa, the West Indies and the Mediterranean entering and leaving the United Kingdom showed that net immigration from these countries between 1955 and the middle of 1962 might have been of the order of 500,000. Estimates made during the same period by Registrars-General in the United Kingdom of the difference between gross inward and gross outward movements of all persons, including United Kingdom British, indicated a net outward balance of migration until 1957 (when the outward balance was 72,000). In 1958 the trend was reversed, and in that and subsequent years there was a net inward balance, which in 1961 was estimated as 170,000. In the face of this evidence of large-scale immigration, the British Government, albeit with great reluctance, decided to follow most other Commonwealth countries in introducing controls on immigration from the Commonwealth into Britain by means of the Commonwealth Immigrants Act.

Part I of the Act came into force on 1st July 1962 and was due to lapse at the end of 1963 unless renewed (see Chapter 14 of The Commonwealth Relations Office List , 1963 edition). It imposes a control on immigration of all Commonwealth citizens except, broadly speaking, those who were born in the United Kingdom or, being citizens of the United Kingdom and Colonies, hold passports issued by a United Kingdom authority. Wives, and children under sixteen, of residents or holders of employment vouchers issued by the Ministry of Labour (see below) are admitted freely.

Admission may not be refused to persons holding vouchers from the Ministry of Labour (see below), bona fide students or persons who can support themselves without employment (including tourists and visiting business men), except on grounds of health, security or criminal record. Vouchers are not required for purely temporary employment (e.g. by students on vacation or by visitors whose employment is incidental to the main purpose of their visit). Besides their power to refuse admission, immigration officers also have power, intended only for exceptional use, to impose conditions upon the admission of Commonwealth citizens.
Persons who do not require vouchers can, if they wish to be reassured before they leave home that they will be admitted to Britain, apply for 'Entry Certificates' at British posts overseas.
Under the Act, Ministry of Labour vouchers are required by Commonwealth immigrants wishing to work in Great Britain. Special rules apply to those who wish to work in Northern Ireland.
There are three categories of voucher, of which the first two carry priority and can therefore be issued without delay.
Digitized by Google
Control on Immigration
PART II
Category A vouchers for those with definite jobs to come to;
Category B vouchers for those with certain qualifications or skills, e.g. nurses or skilled craftsmen;

Category C vouchers for other persons coming to look for work (among these priority is given to applicants with service in the Armed Forces of the Crown in war or in the United Kingdom Armed Forces since 1945). These are issued on a 'first come, first served' basis to applicants from all over the Commonwealth, at a controlled rate. It was announced at the end of July 1963 that, in order to ensure a reasonable distribution of vouchers throughout the Commonwealth, in future no Commonwealth country would receive more than twenty-five per cent of the Category C vouchers available for issue.

Commonwealth citizens and British Protected Persons residing in the United Kingdom are not required, as are aliens, to register with the police; nor do they require permission to change their employment.

Although Part I of the Act also applies to the Republic of Ireland it was decided when the Act was passed that there should be no control on movement between the United Kingdom and the Republic, but if citizens of the Republic land in Britain from abroad they must under the Act be treated in the same way as Commonwealth citizens. The Republic of Ireland have made complementary regulations similar in scope to the Act.

Parts II and III of the Act came into force on the 31st May 1962, and are permanent legislation. Part II gives power to the Home Secretary to deport Commonwealth citizens, British Protected Persons and citizens of the Republic of Ireland on the recommendation of a Court on conviction of an offence punishable with imprisonment. Persons born in the United Kingdom, or whose fathers were born there or who were naturalized or registered in the United Kingdom, and persons who have lived in Britain for more than five years and persons under seventeen years of age are not liable to deportation. Commonwealth High Commissioners in London are informed as soon as it is known that one of their nationals has been recommended for deportation.

Part III of the Act contains ancillary provisions which, inter alia , extended the period of ordinary residence qualifying a citizen of an independent Commonwealth country for registration, and a British Protected Person for naturalization, as a citizen of the United Kingdom and Colonies from one year to five years.

CHAPTER 15
THE ROYAL VISIT TO AUSTRALIA AND NEW ZEALAND 1963

IT was announced from Buckingham Palace on the 21st June 1962 that The Queen and the Duke of Edinburgh would be spending about a fortnight in New Zealand and about a month in Australia in February and March of the following year, at the invitation of the respective Governments. All arrangements for the Visits were made direct between the Governments concerned and Buckingham Palace.

Her Majesty and His Royal Highness left London on the 31st January 1963 in an aircraft of British Overseas Airways Corporation and, after travelling via Vancouver and Honolulu, paid a short visit on the 1st February to Fiji, where they embarked on the Royal Yacht Britannia , arriving at Waitangi in New Zealand on the 6th February. In the evening of that day they attended the Celebrations commemorating the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840. They then proceeded to Auckland, Wellington, Dunedin and Christchurch, and finally left Christchurch by air on the 18th February for Australia.

The Visit was deliberately planned on a less elaborate scale than that in 1954, in order that formality might be reduced to a minimum and as many persons as possible, and especially children, might see The Queen. In addition to visits to places of interest and various institutions, and sporting or similar events, there were a number of official engagements, of which the most important were the Opening of a Special Session of the New Zealand Parliament, the Waitangi Day Celebrations mentioned above, a meeting of the Privy Council for the swearing in of new members, an Investiture, Government Dinners in Wellington and Christchurch, a Garden Party at Dunedin, and attendance at a Royal Opera Performance at Auckland.

Similarly, in Australia, the arrangements were somewhat less formal than in 1954. After two days in Canberra, where Her Majesty received the Heads of Diplomatic Missions and held a meeting of the Privy Council, She and the Duke of Edinburgh visited South Australia, Victoria, Tasmania, New South Wales and Queensland. In addition to other functions, Investitures were held in all these States.

On the 11th March the Royal Party returned to Canberra, and on the 12th March they attended the Ceremony commemorating the Jubilee of the Founding and Naming of Canberra. Other engagements included an Investiture, a Government Dinner, a Garden Party, a Reception of Commonwealth Representatives and a Luncheon with Commonwealth High Commissioners.

On the 14th March Her Majesty and the Duke of Edinburgh left Canberra for the Northern Territory, visiting for the first time Alice Springs and Darwin, at both of which Investitures were held. On the 17th March they proceeded to Western Australia, and on the 27th March, after visits to a number of places in the State, and various functions in Perth including an Investiture, they flew to Sydney, whence they left in the evening by air. After calling at Fiji, Honolulu and Vancouver, they arrived in London on the 28th March.

CHAPTER 17
THE COMMONWEALTH AND THE BRUSSELS NEGOTIATIONS
(See footnote on page 159)
BACKGROUND TO THE NEGOTIATIONS

ON 31st July 1961 the Prime Minister announced in the House of Commons that the British Government had come to the conclusion that it would be right for Britain to make a formal application under Article 237 of the Treaty of Rome, with a view to joining the European Economic Community (E.E.C.) if satisfactory arrangements could be made to meet the special needs of Britain, of the Commonwealth and of the European Free Trade Association.

For several years before that announcement, Commonwealth Governments had been in frequent consultation with each other about the political and economic consequences of the establishment of the E.E.C. and of the threatened division of Western Europe as a result of the subsequent failure of the negotiations for a comprehensive European Free Trade Area. Their concern over these matters was, for example, expressed in the communiqué of the meeting of Commonwealth Prime Ministers in London in May 1960 (quoted on page 116 of the 1961 edition of The Commonwealth Relations Office List ). One main cause of concern was the possibility that as a result of the adoption of 'inward-looking' policies by European countries, aiming at a greater degree of self-sufficiency, Commonwealth countries outside Europe would find an important market for their products less ready to buy them. Commonwealth countries producing tropical commodities, particularly, feared the consequences of the adoption by the E.E.C. as a whole of the preferential treatment accorded by the individual members of the Community to their dependencies associated with the Community under Part IV of the Treaty of Rome. Another cause of concern was the effect on the Commonwealth as a whole of any relative reduction in British economic strength or loss of political influence as a result of being outside the European Community.

In his announcement to the House of Commons on 31st July 1961 the Prime Minister made it clear that this was a political as well as an economic issue; the Treaty of Rome, although it was concerned with economic matters, had an important political objective, namely to promote unity and stability in Europe. He believed that it was both Britain's duty and Britain's interest to contribute towards that strength by securing the closest possible unity within Europe. Britain's contribution to the Commonwealth would, in his view, be enhanced and not reduced if Europe united. On 2nd August 1961 the Prime Minister moved in the House of Commons 'that this House supports the decision of Her Majesty's Government to make formal application under Article 237 of the Treaty of Rome . . .'. In doing so he emphasized that in his view it would be wrong to regard Britain's Commonwealth and European interests as conflicting; basically, he said, they were complementary. He further stressed that it was vital to do nothing that would damage the Commonwealth economically or
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
Brussels Negotiations

politically. He went on to refer to the great advantage to all the partners of the system of free entry and preferences although, he pointed out, their impact had been reduced over recent years with the changing pattern of trade. He concluded:—'Nevertheless we recognize to the full our duty and our obligations to the Commonwealth. In the words of the Motion, our aim in these negotiations is to make satisfactory arrangements to meet the special interests of the Commonwealth, particularly, of course, in the economic field'.

In the ensuing negotiations a very great part of Britain's effort was devoted to seeking to safeguard Commonwealth interests. Most of the products of the other Commonwealth countries enter Britain free of duty or other restrictions. Nearly all goods imported from other Commonwealth countries which do face a tariff in Britain enjoy preferential treatment, as do many other Commonwealth products. Britain's trade relations with certain Commonwealth countries are enshrined in trade agreements providing for mutual rights and obligations, including guaranteed margins of preference on specified commodities. Once established for any country the same treatment is given to all other Commonwealth countries even if they have no trade agreement with Britain, and do not give British goods preferential treatment over foreign goods, or free entry. If Britain were to have joined the E.E.C. without any special arrangements, Commonwealth countries would have been faced very rapidly with loss of the preferences given to their products in Britain and also, in many cases, with positive tariffs in place of their previous free entry. On some important raw materials there would in fact have been no change in the present situation, as both the British and the Common External Tariff of the E.E.C. allow free entry from all sources. Examples are raw cotton, wool and jute, natural rubber, unwrought copper, certain metallic ores, gold and diamonds.
COURSE OF NEGOTIATIONS—GENERAL

On 10th October 1961 the Lord Privy Seal opened the formal negotiations with the E.E.C. with a statement on behalf of the British Government (Cmnd. 1565). In this statement he emphasized the importance of the Commonwealth aspects of the negotiations. He pointed to the Commonwealth's 'essential contribution to the strength and stability of the world', and argued that it was in the interests of all the parties to the negotiations that nothing should be done which would be likely to damage the essential interests of its Member Countries. He explained that Commonwealth trade was one of the strongest elements in maintaining the Commonwealth association; and that, although most of the other Commonwealth countries had sought to enlarge the variety of their production and the range of their markets, the British market was still of great importance to the economies of most of them. He then suggested that the problems involved could best be considered in certain categories. These categories, and the solutions discussed for each during the negotiations, are set out in the paragraphs that follow.
LESS DEVELOPED COUNTRIES

Mr. Heath said that the British Government would like to see the less developed Member countries of the Commonwealth, and the British dependent territories, given the opportunity, should they so wish, to enter into association with the
F
Digitized by Google
Brussels Negotiations
PART II

Community on the same terms as those which would in future be available to the countries and territories already associated by virtue of Part IV of the Treaty. He recognized that some of the Commonwealth countries might prefer different arrangements, and suggested as possible alternative courses the maintenance unimpaired of their rights of access to the British market (as had been done, e.g. for Morocco's trade with France by means of a protocol to the Treaty of Rome); or the consideration of the problems arising on individual commodities, and perhaps the elimination of the duties in the common tariff on some commodities, such as tea and cocoa, which were of particular importance to Commonwealth countries.

As the negotiations proceeded, the Community agreed that association on the same terms as those which their existing associates would enjoy (these were being re-negotiated between the Community and their associates concurrently with the Community's negotiations with Britain) should be offered to the independent Commonwealth countries of Africa and the Caribbean, and to nearly all British dependent territories outside Europe. Special arrangements were negotiated for the few remaining dependent territories, although at the time the negotiations were suspended complete agreement had not been obtained on all of them. Association would have resulted in eventual free entry into the whole of the enlarged Community of almost all the exports of the countries concerned, and also access to the European Development Fund. The Governments of Sierra Leone and Trinidad and Tobago made it known that, if Britain joined the E.E.C., they would wish to associate themselves with the Community. Ghana, Nigeria and the East African countries made it known, however, that association would not be acceptable to them. Nigeria and the East African countries requested that the possibility of trade agreements between the E.E.C. and them should be considered. It was agreed at Brussels that the opportunity to associate with the E.E.C. should remain open to the countries which did not want to accept it initially, and that the enlarged Community would declare its readiness to negotiate commercial agreements with them. It was also agreed that the common external tariff should be applied to these countries' exports to Britain by very gradual stages, and that the tariff on tropical hardwoods (of great significance to Ghana and Nigeria) should be eliminated. There was, in reaching these agreements, a general understanding that the Community would work towards tropical commodity stabilization agreements.

As regards India, Pakistan and Ceylon, the central point in the arrangements agreed upon at Brussels was that the enlarged Community would aim to conclude comprehensive trade agreements with them, at the latest by the end of 1966. The object of these trade agreements would be to develop trade and so to maintain, and as much as possible increase, their foreign currency earnings and to facilitate their development plans. In addition, agreement was reached on the elimination of the tea duties (of special concern to India and Ceylon); on a special interim régime for imports of cotton textiles from India and Pakistan (to last until the proposed trade agreements were made, or the end of 1966); on a particularly slow time-table for applying the common tariff to other imports from these countries into Britain; and on the suspension of duties on certain goods. Proposals made by the British Government for the elimination of duties on other goods (heavy jute goods, East India kips, hand-knotted carpets, coir mats and matting) were not found acceptable by the E.E.C., and no conclusion had been reached when the negotiations broke down.

CHAPTER 17
Brussels Negotiations
MANUFACTURES FROM CANADA, AUSTRALIA AND NEW ZEALAND

In his opening statement, Mr. Heath recognised that indefinite and unlimited continuation of free entry into Britain for imports of manufactures from Canada, Australia and New Zealand (nearly all of which entered Britain free of duty or other restriction) might not be regarded as compatible with the development of the common market. The arrangement worked out in the course of the negotiations provided for alignment of the British tariff on such goods with the common external tariff of the E.E.C. by a slow time-table (slower than that provided in the Treaty of Rome as it stood, but not so slow as that agreed during the Brussels negotiations in respect of imports from the less developed Commonwealth countries); there was also provision for consultation between the Community and these three countries to examine the development of the Community's trade with them during the alignment of the British tariff.
INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS

A substantial number of industrial materials presented no problems because neither the British nor the E.E.C. tariff had duties on them, irrespective of their source. In a few other cases (e.g. asbestos and sisal) the E.E.C. tariff was zero, while Britain afforded Commonwealth goods free entry with a preference over foreign goods; adoption of the E.E.C. tariff in these cases would thus have left free entry unaffected, but would have involved loss of the preference. There were, however, a number of items in this area on which adoption of the common external tariff by Britain would involve the imposition of duties on supplies from Commonwealth countries. The most important of these were aluminium, wood-pulp, newsprint, lead and zinc. On these commodities, and on certain others, the British Government sought the elimination of the duties in the common external tariff. Agreement was reached on various ways of dealing with the problem in respect of certain of the less important items. It was also agreed that Britain would be able to import her requirements of woodpulp free of duty from any source under an existing protocol executed by the Members of the Community when they fixed the rate of duty on wood pulp in the common tariff. On the other major items agreement had not been reached when the negotiations were terminated, although some moves had been made from each side towards a settlement.
TEMPERATE FOODSTUFFS

The question of the treatment of temperate foodstuffs was of particular importance in respect of Canada, Australia and New Zealand. In the negotiations these problems were proposed to be dealt with by a two-fold agreement, relating partly to the longer term and partly to a transitional period. The central point in the arrangements proposed for the longer term was agreement between the Governments which would form the enlarged Community that they would take an early initiative to secure world-wide agreements for the principal agricultural products, thus recognising the enlarged Community's responsibility as the most important food importer in the world. It was agreed that the points to be covered in such agreements would include the price and production policy to be followed by the exporting and importing countries; the minimum and maximum quantities to enter international trade; stockpiling policy; and the special aspects of trade with developing countries. The purpose would be to seek to work out the
Digitized by Google
Brussels Negotiations
PART II

most suitable structure of international trade in agricultural products in order to ensure an agreed balance between the interests of consumers and of producers; and to meet in particular the development of requirements and outlets in the different parts of the world. It was further agreed that the Community's future price policy was particularly important since it would largely determine the volume of production and thus the outlet opportunities for exporting countries. Thus the Community, in taking appropriate measures to raise the individual earnings of those engaged in agriculture in the Community by ensuring the rational development of agricultural production, would also endeavour to contribute to a harmonious development of world trade, including a satisfactory level of trade between itself and third countries, including Commonwealth countries. It was also agreed to state explicitly that the policy which the enlarged Community intended to pursue would offer reasonable opportunities in its markets for exports of temperate foodstuffs from non-member countries. If it proved impracticable to achieve world-wide agreements, the Community would be ready to conclude agreements for the stated purposes with those countries who wished to do so and in particular with Commonwealth countries.

As regards the transitional period, final agreement was not reached on the details but a framework was worked out for the treatment which could be applied to the individual commodities of importance to the Commonwealth for which the Members of the Community would be giving each other preferences over outside suppliers during this period. The Members of the Community stated their intention to ensure that the operation of these preferences inside the Community would not lead to sudden and considerable alterations in trade patterns; and that if these were to occur the Community would review the operation of the preferences in consultation with Commonwealth countries; it was also agreed in principle that imports from the Commonwealth into the United Kingdom should have the benefit of some of the intra-Community preference on a decreasing scale.
NEW ZEALAND

The E.E.C. Ministers taking part in the negotiations recognized the particular difficulties affecting New Zealand because of its high degree of dependence on the sales of agricultural products to the British market, and expressed readiness to consider special provisions to deal with these difficulties. No agreement had been reached on this question when the negotiations were terminated.
PROCESSED FOODSTUFFS

Consideration was also given to the treatment to be accorded to processed foodstuffs imported into Britain from Commonwealth countries, particularly from Canada, Australia and New Zealand. For most of these it was agreed that a particularly slow time-table should be adopted for the application of the common tariff by Britain.
REPUBLIC OF CYPRUS

The Government of the Republic of Cyprus applied in December 1962 for the opening of negotiations with the E.E.C. under Article 238 of the Treaty of Rome with a view to reaching an agreement for the association of Cyprus with the Community. Mr. Heath expressed the hope to the E.E.C. Ministers that these
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
Brussels Negotiations

negotiations would be held as soon as practicable and that an agreement could be reached which would be fully satisfactory to the Republic of Cyprus and to the Community. He suggested that if the negotiations had not been completed by the time that Britain might join the Community, arrangements should be made to preserve the present position for exports from Cyprus to Britain pending the completion of the Article 238 negotiations.
COMMONWEALTH CONSULTATION

As stated earlier in this article, Britain's position in relation to the E.E.C. had been for several years the subject of consultation with other Commonwealth Governments, bilaterally and collectively. The Commonwealth Finance Ministers recorded in the communiqué of the September 1960 meeting of the Commonwealth Economic Consultative Council that:

'The Council discussed the economic problems in relation to Commonwealth trade and investment arising from the creation of the European Economic Community and the European Free Trade Association. United Kingdom Ministers expressed their concern at the possibility of growing economic division in Europe. They gave an account of the recent discussions in which they had been engaged, making it clear that no negotiations between the two groups had yet begun. They stated, however, that the United Kingdom Government would be prepared to examine and discuss proposals for a satisfactory solution.

The Council recognized the importance of political and economic unity in Western Europe. It was accepted that in any negotiations that take place the essential interests of Commonwealth countries should be safeguarded and full account taken of the continuing importance of intra-Commonwealth trade.'

The British Government continued to keep the other Commonwealth Governments in touch with developments in their discussions with the members of the E.E.C., and in June and July 1961 four Cabinet Ministers visited all the capitals of the then Member countries of the Commonwealth (the Minister of State for Colonial Affairs visited the West Indies) for further discussions with their Governments, in the light of which the British Government would further consider their policy towards the E.E.C. The joint statements issued at the conclusion of these visits were published as a White Paper in July 1961 (Cmnd. 1449). During these discussions it was agreed that the British Government and the other Commonwealth Governments should continue to keep in close touch on these problems.
In the House of Commons on 2nd August 1961 the Prime Minister included in his speech moving the Resolution on the opening of negotiations an assurance that:
'We shall consult the Commonwealth countries at every level and at all stages'.
This assurance was implemented in the following principal ways, in addition to the normal diplomatic exchanges of view between the British and other Commonwealth Governments:
(i) collective discussion by Commonwealth Finance Ministers at Accra in September 1961 and by Commonwealth Prime Ministers at London in September 1962;
Digitized by Google
Brussels Negotiations
PART II

(ii) numerous exchanges of messages and visits between British and other Commonwealth Ministers; (iii) detailed discussions with Commonwealth officials before the negotiations began; (iv) meetings at Brussels at which the Lord Privy Seal and officials on the British Delegation to the Brussels Conference discussed with other Commonwealth representatives the proceedings in the ministerial and other sessions of the Conference; (v) meetings at London after each ministerial session of the Conference, at which the Lord Privy Seal discussed the proceedings with Commonwealth High Commissioners; (vi) meetings of the Commonwealth Liaison Committee at London, at which officials from the British Delegation to the Brussels Conference discussed the proceedings at Brussels with the members of the Committee.

It was accepted both by the British Government and the other Commonwealth Governments that the responsibility for deciding whether to join the Community, and on what terms, must rest on the British Government and could not be shared with other Commonwealth Governments. The British Government's object was to keep them informed of the progress of the negotiations and to ensure that they had a full opportunity to comment on the arrangements contemplated, so that when forming their own conclusions the British Government could take account of the views of the other Commonwealth Governments.
AFTER THE BREAKDOWN

On 29th January 1963 the Brussels negotiations broke down as a result of the action of the French Government (the proceedings of the final meetings were reported to the House of Commons by the Lord Privy Seal on 30th January 1963—Hansard Cols. 941 and following). The way to British membership of the Community was thus barred, though the problems connected with Britain's relations with it remained at least as important as before the negotiations began. Some of the concepts thrown up during the negotiations to meet Commonwealth problems remained valid as a basis for further consideration. The British Government continued to seek international agreements on certain temperate foodstuffs and other commodities. The European Commission indicated their belief that some elements of the agreements envisaged for India, Pakistan and Ceylon could be preserved; in a lecture in Delhi on 6th April 1963 the President of the European Commission referred, in this connection, to the possibility of comprehensive trade agreements with the Community. In March 1963 a ministerial mission from the Governments of Tanganyika, Uganda and Kenya visited Brussels and other E.E.C. capitals to exchange information on economic relations between the East African countries and the E.E.C.; the mission indicated their desire for an agreement with the Community which would be distinct from that to be contained in the Convention of Association between the E.E.C. and the existing Associates. On 2nd April 1963 the Prime Minister of Nigeria announced that Nigeria was accrediting an Ambassador to the Community, and intended to seek to negotiate a commercial agreement with the E.E.C. Also at the beginning of April 1963 it was made known at Brussels that the members of the E.E.C. had agreed that, simultaneously with the signing of the proposed new Convention of Association between the E.E.C. and the ex-
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
Brussels Negotiations

isting 'Part Four' Associates, the Community would declare their readiness to negotiate with less developed countries (especially in Africa) wishing to do so for agreements providing either for association under the new Convention; or for other association agreements relating primarily to trade; or for other agreements to develop trade between the E.E.C. and the other countries concerned. The Community also decided in April to propose a simultaneous suspension of the E.E.C. and United Kingdom tariffs on tea and certain types of hardwood. The British Government (in consultation with the Commonwealth Governments concerned) welcomed the proposal and, after negotiations on the details in May/June 1963, it was agreed that the suspensions would be brought into effect on 1st January 1964.

Questions affecting Commonwealth trade in the light of, among other things, developments in Europe, were discussed by Commonwealth Trade Ministers at a meeting of the Commonwealth Economic Consultative Council at London on 13th and 14th May 1963. In their communiqué they said:

'The Council agreed on the need for a general expansion of world trade and on the importance of this for all Commonwealth countries. They accordingly welcomed the initiative that had led to the proposed Kennedy Round of trade negotiations and were agreed on the importance of finding in these negotiations solutions to the problems of temperate agricultural products and of the exports of developing countries (including processed and manufactured goods), no less than to the problems of reducing tariffs on industrial products generally. The ability of different Commonwealth countries to contribute to these negotiations would vary with their circumstances. In particular the less developed countries would not be able to afford reciprocal tariff concessions. All Commonwealth Governments were however determined to play a constructive role in the discussions.'

The Council emphasised the need to solve the problems created by the restrictions imposed by many industrialised countries on access for primary products and processed and manufactured products of particular interest to the less developed countries. They further stressed the importance of measures to assist these countries' trade and the necessity of seeking appropriate measures to facilitate the diversification of their economies and to strengthen their export capacity. They agreed that urgent steps should be taken in the G.A.T.T. to this end, and that they would give their full support to the work of the Preparatory Committee of the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development, and to the Conference itself.

This is only a summary account of those aspects of the negotiations which affected the independent Commonwealth. Fuller details of the proposals made by the British Government and of the arrangements which emerged in the course of the negotiations can be found in the White Papers presented to Parliament by the Lord Privy Seal (Cmnd. 1565, 1805, 1838, 1847, 1882 and 1910), in statements made by him in the House of Commons on several occasions and in the debate on the breakdown of the negotiations (on 11th and 12th February 1963). The Commission of the European Economic Community have also published their account of the course of the negotiations in 'Report to the European Parliament on the state of the negotiations with the United Kingdom' (26th February 1963).

CHAPTER 18
COMMONWEALTH CO-OPERATION IN TELECOMMUNICATIONS

IN his article 'The Modern Commonwealth' in the 1963 edition of The Commonwealth Relations Office List the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations referred to the links which bind together the Commonwealth. One of these is the Commonwealth telecommunications network which has provided a physical link between Commonwealth countries since the first successful submarine telegraph cable was laid in 1866. In the development of the Commonwealth, rapid and reliable communications have been of basic importance; and the habit of continuous consultation between statesmen and governments, so characteristic an aspect of Commonwealth relations, has only been made possible by the development of telecommunications. As the Professor of British Commonwealth Relations at the Royal Institute of International Affairs said in 1960 'It would not be merely fanciful to describe the British Empire as a web of submarine telegraphic cables'.

But not only has the Commonwealth telecommunications system provided an essential physical link between country and country, it has also, in its operation, given rise to a degree of Commonwealth co-operative action perhaps unparalleled in other fields. From 1901, when it was decided to lay a Commonwealth Pacific telegraph cable, until the present time, when a Commonwealth Pacific telephone cable has been newly completed, there has never been a time when there has not been a Commonwealth telecommunications committee in existence nor a time when either a part or the whole of the Commonwealth telecommunications system has not been jointly owned or jointly controlled by a group of Commonwealth countries. As far as can be foreseen, this co-operation in telecommunications affairs is likely to continue. 1964 will see the start of another Commonwealth telephone cable, from Australia to Malaysia and Hong Kong, to be owned jointly by five Commonwealth governments; and discussions are already taking place to consider to what extent it will be possible to continue Commonwealth telecommunications co-operation in the satellite era.

The first long-distance submarine telegraph cable to be successfully operated was laid between the British Isles and Newfoundland by an Anglo-American Company in 1866. By 1872 cable communication between Britain and Australia, via Suez and India, had been opened; and not many more years were to pass before cables had reached almost every part of what was then the British Empire. However one gap remained: the gap across the Pacific from Canada to New Zealand and Australia. The need to fill this gap appears first to have been brought to the official attention of the British government by the High Commissioner for Canada in 1886 in a letter which enclosed the plans of Sandford Fleming, engineer-in-chief of the Canadian Pacific Railway and of the trans-Canada telegraph. Fleming's ideas, which included the laying of a comprehensive government-owned Empire cable touching no point of foreign soil, were among the matters discussed by the Colonial Conferences held in London in 1887 and
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 18
Co-operation in Telecommunications

in Ottawa in 1894, and also at Postal and Telegraph Conferences in Wellington and Sydney. Finally, in 1896, the Colonial Secretary, Mr. Joseph Chamberlain, appointed a Pacific Cable Committee consisting of the Canadian High Commissioner, a Canadian Privy Councillor, the Agents-General of the Colonies of New South Wales and Victoria, and a Treasury representative, under the chairmanship of Lord Selborne. This Committee met in 1898, heard evidence from witnesses, including Sandford Fleming, and in the following year reported to Parliament that the scheme for a Pacific cable, touching at no foreign point, was practicable and desirable; that the cable should be laid and owned by the interested governments; and should be managed by a Committee representing those governments. This proposal was opposed by the telegraph companies, on the grounds that the state should not encroach on the preserves of private enterprise, but was accepted by the Colonial Secretary who declared that he could see no objection to the extension of state-ownership from internal to external telecommunications, particularly as the telegraph companies were unwilling or unable to lay the cable themselves on what he considered to be reasonable terms. By the Pacific Cable Act of 1901 Parliament endorsed the proposal for this first co-operative government-owned Empire telecommunications venture and set up a Pacific Cable Board to control and manage the construction and working of the cable, with three British, two Canadian and one New Zealand representatives, and two representatives of the three Australian States, New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland. The cost of £2,000,000 was divided as to 5/18ths each by Britain and Canada and as to 1/9th each by New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland and New Zealand. The cable, designed to carry six paid words a minute, was completed in 1902, and was later duplicated. The Pacific Cable Board continued to function until 1928, but Sandford Fleming's wider plans for a government-owned, round-the-world, Empire cable were not to be revived until 1958; the other telegraph cables in the Commonwealth network remaining in the ownership of the telegraph companies.

In 1927 the first short-wave 'beam' wireless telegraph service between Britain and Australia was opened; and it quickly became clear that, unless protective measures were to be taken, cables would not be able to compete with the new method of transmission. To examine the situation which had arisen, to report and to make recommendations, an Imperial Wireless and Cable Conference was called in 1928, attended by representatives of Britain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, the Irish Free State and India, with representatives of the Colonies and Protectorates. This Conference sat for several months; and because 'the telegraph service is not only a matter of common interest, but possibly the link on which, more than any other, the several parts of the Empire depend for mutual intercourse and mutual understanding' came to the conclusion that it was important that the Commonwealth cable system should continue in use. The Committee was influenced in this decision by a desire to prevent Commonwealth telecommunications falling into foreign hands and, in particular, by a desire to preserve the cable route from Britain to Australia via Canada which passed through no foreign point. They therefore proposed, and even helped to negotiate, a merger of the cable and wireless interests; and, as a result of their recommendations, a single operating company, later to be known as Cable and Wireless Ltd., was set up. To this were transferred not only the telecommunications assets of the two main groups of cable and wireless companies but also much of the wireless equipment of the British Post
Digitized by Google
Co-operation in Telecommunications
PART II

Office and the cables of the Pacific Cable Board. Although the Company remained a British company, with a duty towards its shareholders, an Imperial Communications Advisory Committee was set up, composed of representatives of those countries which had attended the Conference; and this Advisory Committee was required to lay down the policy to be followed by the Company. At first Cable and Wireless Ltd. did not in all cases completely control the commercial telecommunications interests in the Dominions and in India but, with the approval of the governments, the Company gradually took over those interests; and it could then be said that the Imperial Communications Advisory Committee, which was a standing Commonwealth committee in London, was responsible for the policy of the Commonwealth telecommunications network.
In 1937 there was a further Commonwealth Conference which succeeded in making recommendations which enabled the intra-Commonwealth rates to be reduced to a uniform charge of 1s. 3d. a word.

At a Commonwealth Conference held in Australia in 1942 it was agreed that the Imperial Communications Advisory Committee should cease to be a standing committee in London but should meet once a year; and that its name should be changed to the Commonwealth Communications Council. This Council in 1944 began to give consideration to the future of the Commonwealth telecommunications network and made certain proposals. These were not entirely acceptable to the British Government which sent Lord Reith to the Dominions and to India; and on his return a Commonwealth Telecommunications Conference was called in 1945, and was attended by representatives of Britain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, India and Southern Rhodesia. This Conference recommended that Cable and Wireless Ltd. should be nationalized; that Britain, the Dominions and India should each assume the assets of the Company in their respective territories; and that a Commonwealth Telecommunications Board should be set up whose functions should be mainly advisory but substantially wider than those of the Commonwealth Communications Council. These recommendations were accepted; and in 1948, 'in order to promote and co-ordinate the efficiency and development of the telecommunications services of the British Commonwealth and Empire' a Commonwealth Telegraphs Agreement was signed. Under this Agreement, the recommendations of the Conference concerning the nationalization of the assets of Cable and Wireless Ltd. were accepted; in each country the assets were to be put into the hands of a National Body; the National Bodies collectively were to be responsible for operating the Commonwealth telecommunications system; but both the Partner Governments and the National Bodies were to be advised by a Commonwealth Telecommunications Board, a standing committee with headquarters in London, on the formulation and execution of their joint telecommunications policy, including the fixing of rates, the co-ordination of the development of the Commonwealth telecommunications system, and other matters. In addition, a 'wayleave' scheme was eventually adopted under which each National Body kept its own revenue but was responsible for the same percentage of the costs of the whole system as the percentage which its own revenue formed of the total revenue of the system. Under this Agreement, which was in 1963 modified by a supplemental Agreement, the telegraph cable and wireless system of the Commonwealth is still operated on a co-operative basis by the Commonwealth Partner Governments and is owned by the National Bodies. As dependent territories become independent

dent, they are invited to accede to the Agreement and to appoint a Member to the Board.

After the 1939 war, the fact that Commonwealth telecommunications were operated co-operatively by Commonwealth countries led to another form of joint Commonwealth action: the negotiation by certain Commonwealth governments, on behalf of the Commonwealth, of an Agreement with the United States of America. During the war it had not been possible to maintain the principle that Cable and Wireless Ltd. should alone provide the telecommunications service of the Commonwealth; and a number of circuits were opened between Commonwealth countries and the United States. In December 1945 a Conference was held in Bermuda with Britain, Canada, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, India and Southern Rhodesia on the one side of the table and the United States on the other; and an Agreement was signed fixing the rates which should be charged for messages between the Commonwealth system and the United States. In 1949 there was another Commonwealth/United States telecommunications meeting to revise the rates; and Supplemental Agreements were signed between the United States and the Commonwealth Governments after this meeting and again in 1952.

Just as in 1928 a Commonwealth Conference was called to consider the impact of a new means of telecommunications, the 'beam' wireless, so in 1958 a Commonwealth Conference was called to consider the impact of another new invention, the submarine telephone cable. The telegraph cables had not, of course, been able to transmit the spoken word; and long-distance telephone conversations could only be carried by wireless. Owing to atmospheric conditions and other factors radio-telephony is not always satisfactory; and for many years experiments had been made to try to send the spoken word by cable. The main difficulty in doing this was the fact that speech signals could not be made sufficiently powerful to travel the long distances involved. This difficulty was resolved by the use of amplifiers, known as repeaters, incorporated in the cables at intervals of some 26 miles. The first long-distance submarine telephone cable was laid between Britain and Newfoundland with onward links to the United States and Canada. In an age of initials this cable, the first Trans-Atlantic Telephone Cable, is known as TAT I. Because of difficulties in laying a cable containing rigid repeaters, the repeaters in this cable were made flexible and would only work one way; so in fact two cables had to be laid, to provide two-way conversations. Meanwhile the British Post Office had invented a lightweight cable with the strength unit in the centre instead of, as heretofore, in the outer, armoured skin. When it became apparent that the TAT cable would create a demand which it could not fill, the British Post Office and the Canadian Overseas Telecommunication Corporation made an agreement to lay a new-type cable between Britain and Canada, to be known as the Canadian Trans-Atlantic Cable (CANTAT). This cable, which carries 60 simultaneous two-way conversations, was opened by Her Majesty The Queen in December 1961.

The 1958 Conference was attended by representatives of an increased number of Commonwealth countries: Britain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, India, Pakistan, Ceylon, Ghana, Malaya and the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland. These representatives unanimously agreed that it was commercially and politically desirable that a comprehensive Commonwealth telephone cable system should be laid round the world. They estimated that the cost would be £80 million and that it would be profitable; and they recommended that it

should be constructed in sections, the capital for each being provided by those Commonwealth countries which were interested in it. The Conference was, however, unable to agree that telephone cables should form part of the Commonwealth Telecommunications system for the purposes of the financial arrangements under the Commonwealth Telegraphs Agreement, 1948 (the wayleave scheme); and in consequence the Commonwealth telephone cable system, at present, remains separate from the telegraph cable and wireless system.

The Report was approved by the Commonwealth Trade and Economic Conference held in Montreal later in the same year and the recommendations were accepted by Commonwealth Governments. The initiative for building the second sector of the cable (CANTAT being the first) came from Australia whose Government invited other Commonwealth Governments to a Conference in Sydney in 1959 to discuss the laying of a Pacific telephone cable. The invitation was accepted by Britain, Canada and New Zealand; and the Report of the Conference, like the Report of the earlier Pacific Cable Committee, concluded that the construction of the cable was desirable and practicable, that its capital cost of £27 million should be jointly borne, and that the cable should be jointly owned by the four Governments, who should appoint a Management Committee to superintend its construction. This Commonwealth Cable across the Pacific (COMPAC) has 80 circuits. It reached New Zealand from Australia in July 1962 and was expected to be in full service in December 1963. It will land at Hawaii, to connect with United States' systems, but, except for this, will, with CANTAT, provide as did the 1902 cable, a direct link between Britain and Australia which does not touch at any foreign point.

The next sector of the cable to be planned was from Australia to Singapore and Malaya, with a spur to Hong Kong. Planned at a Conference in Kuala Lumpur in July 1961, a Conference attended by representatives of Britain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, India, Pakistan, Ceylon, Malaya, Singapore, Hong Kong, Sarawak, North Borneo and Brunei, the cable, to be known as the South-East Asia Commonwealth Cable (SEACOM), will cost £19 million to be shared by Britain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Malaya and Singapore (Malaysia); and it is expected that the first sector, from Hong Kong to Singapore via Sabah, will be completed by the beginning of 1965. Again there is a Management Committee composed of representatives of the Partner Governments.

Whether more sectors of the Commonwealth telephone cable will be laid must depend on the progress that is made with communications by means of satellites. Even before the American satellites, Relay and Telstar, had shown that this new form of telecommunications was practicable, the Commonwealth had met together in a Conference in London in 1962 to consider what part it should play in the satellite communications era which was believed to be imminent. The Conference, which at that stage could only be exploratory, considered that communication by satellite was likely to be feasible and that any commercial system should provide as wide a coverage of countries, and as great a degree of flexibility, as possible. What part the Commonwealth should play in this system has not yet been decided.

Thus Commonwealth co-operation in telecommunications which began with the Pacific Telegraph cable in 1901 has developed continuously over the years. Of the 17 Members of the Commonwealth, 10 are now parties to the Commonwealth Telegraphs Agreements of 1948 and 1963. Through their National Bodies they own the greater part of the Commonwealth telegraph cable and wireless

system, and operate it jointly with the advice of the Commonwealth Telecommunications Board. Directly or indirectly all Members of the Commonwealth, except Pakistan, and all other Commonwealth countries participate in the financial arrangement known as the wayleave scheme. In addition, two separate groups of Commonwealth countries jointly own and operate the two completed sectors of the Commonwealth telephone cable, and the third sector will similarly be jointly owned and constructed by a third group of Commonwealth countries. Plans are already under discussion to co-ordinate these sectors of the telephone cable with the telegraph cable and wireless network and to introduce a unified financial arrangement. And there is still scope for the Commonwealth to play its part in the satellite era.

CHAPTER 20
CANADA
GOVERNOR-GENERAL AND COMMANDER-IN-CHIEF
His Excellency Major-General The Rt. Hon. Georges P. Vanier, DSO, MC, CD
GENERAL

CANADA occupies the northern half of the North American Continent with the exception of Alaska, which is part of the United States, Greenland which belongs to Denmark, and the small islands of St. Pierre and Miquelon off the coast of Newfoundland, which belong to France. In latitude the country stretches from Middle Island in Lake Erie, at $41^\circ 41'$ N., to Cape Columbia on Ellesmere Island, at $83^\circ 07'$ N. It thus includes the islands north of the mainland known as the Canadian Arctic Archipelago and those in the extreme north, lying north of the M'Clure Strait-Viscount Melville Sound-Barrow Strait-Lancaster Sound water passage, known as the Queen Elizabeth Islands. Other islands of importance are Vancouver Island and the Queen Charlotte Islands off the West Coast; the island of Newfoundland forming part of the Province of Newfoundland; the Province of Prince Edward Island; Cape Breton Island forming part of the Province of Nova Scotia; Grand Manan and Campobello Islands forming part of the Province of New Brunswick; Anticosti Island and the Magdalen group included in the Province of Quebec. Canada is the largest country in the Western Hemisphere and the second largest country in the world, comprising an area computed at 31,851,809 square miles of land and fresh water, over forty times the area of Britain. Its inland waters are very extensive, constituting about 7.6 per cent of the total area of the country. The Great Lakes are the outstanding lakes of the country, their total Canadian area being almost 36,000 square miles. Other large lakes ranging in area from 9,500 to 12,300 square miles are Lake Winnipeg, Great Slave Lake and Great Bear Lake. In addition there are innumerable lakes scattered over that major portion of Canada lying within the Canadian Shield; in an area of 6,094 square miles south and east of Lake Winnipeg there are 3,000 lakes. Eastern Canada is dominated by the Great Lakes-St. Lawrence system which drains an area of about 678,000 square miles and forms an unequalled navigable inland waterway through a region rich in natural and industrial resources. From the head of Lake Superior to the entrance to the Gulf of St. Lawrence the distance is 2,280 miles. In the mid-west two main branches of the Saskatchewan River, tributary to the Nelson flowing into Hudson Bay, drain one of Canada's great agricultural regions and are now the bases of important irrigation projects. North-westward, one of the world's longest rivers, the Mackenzie, flows 2,635 miles to the Arctic Ocean and drains an area in the three westernmost provinces of approximately 700,000 square miles.

The Canadian federal state was established by the British North America Act, 1867, and now consists of ten Provinces and two Territories. The Provinces, with the dates on which they joined the Confederation, are Ontario (1867), Quebec (1867), Nova Scotia (1867), New Brunswick (1867), Manitoba (1870),

British Columbia (1871), Prince Edward Island (1873), Saskatchewan (1905), Alberta (1905) and Newfoundland (1949). The Territories are the Northwest Territories (1870) and the Yukon Territory (1898). The Northwest Territories were divided in 1920 into the Districts of Mackenzie, Keewatin and Franklin.

As recorded at the Census of 1961, the population of Canada was 18,238,247, of whom 70 per cent were classed as urban dwellers; 53 per cent lived in or on the fringes of urban centres having a population of 30,000 or more and 12 per cent lived on farms. Of the total population, 34 per cent were under 15 years of age and 58.4 per cent were in the working age group 15 to 64 years; 7.6 per cent were over 64 years of age. The two basic ethnic groups in the Canadian population are the British Isles group and the French. In 1961 43.8 per cent of the population belonged to the former group and 30.4 per cent to the latter. The next largest ethnic origin was German with 5.8 per cent of the total, Ukrainian with 2.6 per cent, Italian with 2.5 per cent and Netherlandic with 2.4 per cent. Asians made up only 0.7 per cent of the population.

The native peoples of Canada, the Indians and Eskimos, together made up only 1.2 per cent of the total population. The Indian population numbered 191,709, including all persons with a paternal ancestor of Indian race who have chosen to remain under Indian legislation. About 74 per cent of the Indians live on reserves having a total area of 5,918,279 acres. Those living off the reserves are mainly in the Northwest Territories where reserves have not been set aside for them. In the northern and other outlying areas, hunting, fishing and trapping remain an important means of livelihood for them but in the more settled areas many Indians have fitted into the economy of the communities in which they live in a wide range of occupations. Subject to special provisions in the Indian Act, all laws of general application are applicable to Indians, and they may vote in federal elections on the same basis as other citizens and in provincial elections where the electoral laws of the provinces permit. Indian affairs are administered by the Federal Government and are conducted in a manner that will enable the Indians to participate fully in the social and economic life of the country. A wide range of programmes has been brought into effect in the fields of education, economic development, social welfare and community development; nearly 46,600 Indians are enrolled in schools throughout the country.

The affairs of the 11,835 Canadian Eskimos living in the Northwest Territories, northern Quebec and Labrador are also administered by the Federal Government. While many of them still hold to the traditional way of life, an ever-increasing number are making the change from a nomadic existence to regular wage employment. Continued development in the north, coupled with a decrease in some types of game, is resulting in more and more Eskimos settling in modern communities with schools, health and transportation facilities and wage employment opportunities. The Canadian Government is helping this transition by providing such forms of assistance as education and welfare services, vocational training and economic development programmes. About 2,400 Eskimo children now regularly attend schools.

Under the provisions of the British North America Act, 1867, either the English or the French language may be used in debates in the Parliament of Canada and in the Legislature of Quebec and either of these languages may be used by any person or in any Pleading or Process in or issuing from any Court of Canada established under the Act and in or from all or any of the Courts of Quebec. PART III
The National Day of Canada is 1st July, Dominion Day.
THE NATIONAL CAPITAL

Ottawa, designated the national capital of Canada at Confederation in 1867, is situated in an area of great natural beauty at the junction of the Ottawa, Rideau and Gatineau rivers. Although the city remains a self-governing municipality, the Federal Government is carrying out through the medium of the National Capital Commission a Master Plan (approved in 1946), designed to guide the development of the capital's urban area over the following fifty years and to protect and develop the natural beauty of the National Capital District of about 1,800 square miles. The major projects under the Master Plan are concerned with the development of open space, parkways, the relocation of railways, the decentralization of new government building construction, the provision of a green belt around the city, the development of Gatineau Park, a 75,000 acre forest and lake area northwest of Hull, and the conservation of historic buildings and sites in the Laurentian Hills to the north.
HISTORY

Discovery and Exploration. The original inhabitants of North America migrated from Asia across the Bering Strait over twenty-five thousand years ago, gradually dispersing themselves throughout the continent. The first Europeans known to have landed on Canadian shores were the Vikings under Leif Ericson about 1000 A.D., who founded short-lived settlements, probably in Newfoundland or Labrador. Thereafter contact was lost between Europe and the New World.

The re-discovery of North America by Columbus encouraged other mariners to sail westward, among them John Cabot who, in the service of Henry VII, made a landfall in the Gulf of St. Lawrence in 1497. Cabot's reports of the abundance of fish off Newfoundland attracted French, Spanish, Portuguese and English fishermen, who have continued to frequent these fishing grounds ever since. As early as 1543 Jacques Cartier, in the service of France, visited the Gulf of St. Lawrence and sailed up the St. Lawrence River. He visited Indian villages on the sites of present-day Quebec and Montreal. The name 'Canada' may be derived from the Indian word 'Kanata', meaning a town, applied to one of these villages.

Seeking a north-west passage to the Orient, Frobisher in 1576 and Davis in 1585 penetrated into the Frobisher and Davis Strait, and in 1602 Hudson Strait was discovered, and Hudson himself explored Hudson Bay in 1610. But the explorations of Baffin and others eventually persuaded the explorers that there was no suitable north-west passage. It was not until the nineteenth century that the explorations of Parry, Ross and Franklin enabled the passage to be passed by Roald Amundsen in 1906. The Pacific coast of Canada was explored by the Russians from Siberia, and by the Spaniards from Mexico, in the eighteenth century. It was left to Captain Cook to make a more thorough survey in 1778-79 and to Captain George Vancouver to complete his work in 1792-94.

The exploration of the interior of northern North America was impelled by the requirements of the fur trade. Samuel de Champlain of France reached the shores of Lake Huron in 1615 and laid the basis for the exploration of the Great Lakes. Other French traders turned south to the Mississippi or traversed the wilderness north of the Great Lakes to reach the western prairies. The Hudson's Bay Company also sent explorers into the Saskatchewan country. Alexander

CHAPTER 20
Canada
Mackenzie, a Montreal fur trader, was the first man to travel overland across northern North America. He reached the Pacific Coast in 1793.

Settlement. The main English settlements in North America were those along the Atlantic seaboard which later became the United States. Settlements in the northern part of the continent emerged through the need for bases for the fisheries and the fur trade. The English settlements were centred in Newfoundland, but the Hudson's Bay Company, founded by Royal Charter in 1670, claimed trading rights over Rupert's Land, defined as the area whose rivers drained into Hudson Bay. The father of the French empire in North America, Samuel de Champlain, founded Quebec at the narrows of the St. Lawrence in 1608. A riverine colony, New France or Quebec became the base for a chain of fur trading posts that reached south to the Gulf of Mexico (to the landward of the English Atlantic colonies) and westward to the Rocky Mountains.

Until 1663 Quebec was governed autocratically by a trading company; in that year it became a Royal Province, under a Governor to whom was entrusted the general policy of the colony, the direction of its military affairs and its relations with the Indian tribes. A Superior Council also existed with certain administrative powers more formal than real. This system continued until the end of the French régime.

Meanwhile the English North American colonies established along the Atlantic seaboard were growing in population and wealth. Economic rivalry in the fishing and fur trades between the two European empires was re-enforced by dynastic struggles in Europe. Four major wars were fought between 1689 and 1763, each with its North American sphere of operations. By the Treaty of Utrecht which, in 1713, ended the second of these wars, 'France surrendered all claims to Rupert's Land, to Acadia 'within its ancient limits' and to her settlements in Newfoundland, retaining, however, two small islands, St. Pierre and Miquelon (which she still possesses) and some controversial fishing rights which remained in dispute until 1904. France, however, only surrendered that part of Acadia which is now Nova Scotia, keeping that part which is now New Brunswick as well as Isle St. Jean (Prince Edward Island) and Isle Royale (Cape Breton Island) on which was constructed the fortress of Louisburg.

That part of Acadia which was surrendered was renamed Nova Scotia, and possessed few British settlers until the strategic base at Halifax was established in 1749. The fourth war, the Seven Years War, reached its North American culmination in Wolfe's victory at the Plains of Abraham outside Quebec (1759) which led to the conquest of Quebec and Montreal and the fall of the French empire in North America. By the Treaty of Paris of 1763 New France ceased to exist and all French territory east of the Mississippi was transferred to British sovereignty.

From 1763 until the outbreak of the American War of Independence in 1775, the whole of North America to the east of the Mississippi was held by Britain, the various colonies having a population of nearly 2,000,000 persons. In the north was Rupert's Land, under the jurisdiction of the Hudson's Bay Company; Newfoundland, still sparsely inhabited by fishermen; Nova Scotia, including Cape Breton Island and what is now New Brunswick and Prince Edward Island; and the Province of Quebec, comprising the area of the former French settlements along the St. Lawrence and the Great Lakes.
The final partition of North America occurred as a result of the American War of Independence, which in some respects reflected the old economic rivalry
Digitized by Google
Canada
PART III

between French and English in North America. An attempt by the American colonies to invade Quebec was unsuccessful and the Treaty of Paris, 1783, established what was to become the definitive boundary in eastern North America from the St. Croix River in the Bay of Fundy to the Lake of the Woods. Various adjustments were, however, later made in the boundary, notably the Maine-New Brunswick boundary settlement (the Webster-Ashburton treaty) of 1842.

From 1763 to 1774 Quebec was governed in the main by military authority. In the latter year the Quebec Act, passed by the British Parliament, secured for the French colonists the right to retain their language, religion and civil law. Roman Catholics were allowed the free exercise of their religion and were relieved of all civil disabilities. An appointed Council was created to advise the Governor. The Quebec Act, by laying the legal basis for the survival of French culture and institutions in North America, has always been regarded as the Magna Carta of French-Canadian nationalism. Its embodiment of the principle of toleration for non-British elements in the colonies represented the emergence of a policy that was to be highly significant for the later Commonwealth.

The British character of the remaining North American colonies was strengthened as a result of the American Revolution. A considerable migration of Loyalists, perhaps 35,000 in number, moved north to remain under the rule of the Crown. Many of these people went to Nova Scotia, from which Prince Edward Island had been detached in 1769, while others settled in Cape Breton Island (a separate colony from 1784 to 1820 when it again became part of Nova Scotia) or established the new colony of New Brunswick. Still others entered Quebec, the majority settling in the western reaches of the colony. Objecting to the authoritarian rule of the Quebec Act, the Loyalists and other British residents petitioned for representative institutions. In 1791 Parliament passed the Constitutional Act to meet their wishes. Quebec was divided along the Ottawa River into two provinces: Upper Canada (now Ontario) and Lower Canada (Quebec). An elected Assembly was provided for each province, though the Governors, appointed by the Crown, and the nominated Legislative Councils retained control. Thus the Canadas followed Nova Scotia, which had been granted an Assembly in 1758, in taking the first step towards democratic institutions. Assemblies were also authorized for New Brunswick and Prince Edward Island, but Newfoundland, with its large transient fishing population, did not gain a legislature until 1832.

The economic life of the British North American colonies in the early nineteenth century was based on several great staple trades. Fishing, the oldest of these activities, continued to be pursued all along the coasts of the Maritime colonies and Newfoundland and around the Gulf of St. Lawrence. The Napoleonic Wars produced a great demand for timber and a flourishing export in square timbers and naval stores grew up in New Brunswick and Quebec. All the Maritime colonies, but particularly Nova Scotia, built wooden ships for sale or for their own carrying trade. The Canadas exported wheat to Britain and, under the protection of the British preferential system, engaged in a large milling industry. The fur trade continued to be the dominant activity in the vast interior, where the Hudson's Bay Company (which by absorbing rival Montreal fur trading interests in 1821 monopolised trade throughout the West to the Pacific) exercised semi-governmental powers over the traders and the Indian population. Efforts were made to improve the transportation system of the Great Lakes-St. Lawrence route, first by canals and then by railways, in an attempt to channel
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 20
Canada
the export trade of the American mid-West through British North American ports.

The British North American colonies gained a considerable increase in population through the waves of immigration that flowed out from Europe in the 1830s, '40s and '50s. By 1851, the year of the first census, the population of the colonies stood at 2.4 millions. The colonies suffered economic dislocation in 1846, when Britain embarked upon the policy of free trade, and in 1849, when the Navigation Laws were repealed. The search for assured markets turned their eyes to the south, where the United States economy was advancing rapidly during this period. The Governor-General of British North America, Lord Elgin (8th Earl of Elgin and Kincardine), was successful in negotiating a Reciprocity Treaty (the Elgin-Marcy treaty) in 1854. This provided for the free exchange of natural products and opened the Maritime inshore fisheries to American vessels. Although the treaty was abrogated by the United States in 1866, the demands of the Civil War created a large interchange of goods under it, and brought prosperity to British North America.

In Upper and Lower Canada the constitution of 1791 did not prove a success. A struggle soon developed between the elected assembly and the executive composed of the governor and his advisers; this conflict was exacerbated in Lower Canada by friction between the French-speaking majority, who dominated the assembly, and the English minority, whose representatives surrounded the governor. Disputes over the control of finance led to a small and ineffectual rebellion in Upper Canada in 1837 and to more serious uprisings in Lower Canada which lasted into 1838. The Melbourne government in Britain sent Lord Durham to the Canadas to investigate and report on the situation.

Durham was in North America for only five months, but his Report on the Affairs of British North America is one of the great landmarks in the history of Britain's relations with her colonies. His solution of the problem of how to preserve the relationship between Britain and her empire was to urge that the colonies be given self-government in all matters except those, such as foreign relations, regulation of commerce, the disposal of public lands and the determination of constitutions, which then appeared essential for the maintenance of imperial unity. At the same time he recommended the union of Upper and Lower Canada under one government in the hope that this might help to assimilate the French population. His recommendations were partly embodied in the Union Act of 1840, which set up a single Province of Canada. The common government was to consist of the Governor, a nominated Legislative Council and an Assembly of 84 members, 42 to be elected from each part. The new constitution did not correct what Durham saw as the basic weakness of government in British North America—the lack of conformity between the executive and the legislature.

The struggle to establish the practice of responsible or cabinet government in British North America continued for the next eight years. The principle was first achieved in Nova Scotia, following instructions sent to Sir John Harvey, the Lieutenant-Governor, by Lord Grey (3rd Earl Grey), the Colonial Secretary: 'It cannot be too distinctly acknowledged that it is neither possible nor desirable to carry on the government of any of the British provinces in North America in opposition to the opinion of the inhabitants'. As a result Nova Scotia witnessed the accession to office, early in 1848, of a reform ministry which enjoyed the confidence of the majority of the legislature. In Canada the principle was affirmed in 1849 when Lord Elgin, despite riots and the burning of the Parliament
