Nyasaland
Imports, in 1961, were worth £8,702,059, re-exports £174,878 and domestic exports £9,979,883.

Nyasaland Railways operate 273 miles of line, and a boat service on Lake Nyasa. In 1961 the railways carried 973,097 short tons of freight and 548,722 passengers. The main airport is Chileka, 11 miles from Blantyre, with a runway length of 6,072 feet. The total road mileage is 6,184, of which just over 266 miles are bituminous.
HISTORY

The first western records of what is now Nyasaland come from the journals of Portuguese explorers of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, but the archaeological record indicates early settlement by Bantu peoples, dating back to the 'Stamped Ware' culture of the first century A.D.

The modern history of Nyasaland may be said to have begun with the visit by David Livingstone to Lake Nyasa in 1859. British interest in the area was sustained by various missionary societies including the Universities Mission to Central Africa (Anglican), the Church of Scotland and Free Church of Scotland Missions. The country during these early years was occupied by nomadic tribes warring with one another and selling their prisoners to Arab slave-dealers. The missionary pioneers were followed by traders, hunters and farmers. In 1883 a British Consul was established at Blantyre and accredited to the 'Kings and Chiefs of Central Africa'. His successor established himself at Zomba, the present capital. In 1889 a Portuguese expedition came into conflict with one of the African Chiefs, in consequence of which the Acting Consul proclaimed a British Protectorate over the Shire country on 21st September 1889. The Africa Order in Council dated 15th October 1889 included the territory now known as Nyasaland as one of the territories under Her Majesty's protection. In 1891 an Anglo-Portuguese Convention ratified the work of Johnston, Sharpe and others and by the British Central Africa Order in Council, No. 663, dated 11th August 1902, the territories to the west and south of Lake Nyasa and bounded by North-Eastern Rhodesia, German East Africa and the Portuguese territories became known as the British Central Africa Protectorate, the territory being governed by the Consul-General and Commissioner. By the Nyasaland Order in Council dated 6th July 1907 the territory was renamed the Nyasaland Protectorate. A Governor was appointed to replace the Commissioner and Executive and Legislative Councils were set up.
CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT
The first Legislative Council met on 4th September 1907 and an Executive Council of senior officials was established at the same time.

The possibility of associating Nyasaland with one or more of its neighbours had been considered at various times between the two World Wars. Interest revived after the Second World War and in 1951, 1952 and 1953 a series of conferences worked out the implications and detailed organization of a federal form of government for Nyasaland, Southern Rhodesia and Northern Rhodesia. Agreement was reached between all the governments concerned and by the Constitution Order in Council of 1953 (S.I. 1953 No. 1100) the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland came into existence on 3rd September 1953. As a result audit, civil aviation, customs, immigration, income tax, posts and telecommuni-
Digitized by Google
Nyasaland
PART III
cations, prisons, public health and certain categories of education were transferred to the control of the Federal Government.

Further constitutional advance in the territorial sphere followed in 1955 when the Legislative Council was reconstituted to comprise the Governor as President, four ex officio members, seven official members, six non-African members elected by voters on a non-African electoral roll, and five Africans elected by the African Provincial Councils.

The State of Emergency declared in March 1959 as a result of disturbances which broke out all over the country, temporarily precluded the further constitutional advances which had been planned for that year. However, two more African members and two more Officials were introduced into the Legislative Council in August 1959, and two African Members were appointed to the Executive Council in August 1959 for the first time.

In July 1960 a new Constitution was agreed upon at a conference held in London. This came into force in 1961 and provided, for the first time, for direct election of Africans to the Legislative Council, and introduced a higher and lower qualitative franchise for voters. Elections took place in August 1961, resulting in a majority for Dr. H. Kamuzu Banda's Malawi Congress Party. The Executive Council was appointed with three ex officio members, two nominated civil servants and five elected members, all of whom were to be known as Ministers. A conference held in November 1962 was followed by the introduction of the present Constitution on 1st February 1963.

As a result of the Victoria Falls Conference held between 28th June and 3rd July 1963 the dissolution of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland was planned for 31st December 1963. This will result in the reassumption by the Nyasaland Government of the various responsibilities transferred to the Federal Government in 1953.
In September 1963 the British Government announced that Nyasaland would become an independent country on 4th July 1964.
CONSTITUTION
The present Constitution came into effect early in 1963, and was introduced in two stages.

The first stage took effect on the 1st February 1963 and the then existing Constitution was amended to bring into force changes in the composition of the Executive Council, restyled 'Cabinet', and the Legislature, including the appointment of a Prime Minister (Dr. Banda).
The second stage, brought into operation in the late Spring of 1963, had the following main provisions:—

The Governor is required to consult the Cabinet in the exercise of his functions and will normally act in accordance with the advice he receives. He retains reserved powers in the fields of the economy and finance, the public service, public safety and operational control of the police. He is assisted by a Deputy Governor.

The Cabinet which is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly, replaces the former Executive Council and is composed of a Prime Minister, normally not more than eight other Ministers and the Financial Secretary, who is ex officio Minister of Finance. Provision exists under the Constitution for the number of Ministers to be increased if the Prime Minister considers it necessary

Nyasaland

and Her Majesty's Government concur and there is also provision that, at a time to be agreed between the Nyasaland Government and Her Majesty's Government, the Portfolio of Finance can be assumed by an elected member.
The Governor, on the advice of the Prime Minister, has power to appoint Parliamentary Secretaries to assist Ministers.

The Legislative Assembly replaces the former Legislative Council and consists of a Speaker, the Financial Secretary, so long as he is a member of the Cabinet, and, for the present, twenty-eight elected members. Any Minister who is not a member of the Legislative Assembly has the right to attend and take part in the proceedings of the Assembly, but not to vote. The Assembly has a life of not more than five years.

The twenty-eight elected members are elected in single member geographical constituencies on a two-tier qualitative franchise containing members of all races. Twenty of these are elected in one set of constituencies by voters who satisfy the lower qualifications and eight in another set of constituencies by voters who satisfy the higher qualifications. At the 1962 Conference it was agreed that future changes to the franchise could be carried out at a later convenient date after further consultations. The last general election was held in August 1961.

The Constitution also incorporates changes in the Judiciary which were agreed at the 1962 Conference. The appointment of the Chief Justice is made by the Governor after consultation with the Prime Minister. Other judges of the High Court are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Judicial Service Commission.

The Constitution provides for the establishment of Public, Police and Judicial Service Commissions with executive powers. Until a full compensation scheme is brought into operation, the Commissions will be advisory to the Governor in respect of overseas civil servants.
The Constitution also embodies a Bill of Rights.
MINISTERIAL RESPONSIBILITY IN BRITAIN
Responsibility for Nyasaland affairs has since 19th March 1962 been vested in the Central African Office, of which (since October 1963) the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations is in charge.

CHAPTER 41
MALDIVE ISLANDS

THE Maldive Archipelago, 400 miles to the south-west of Ceylon, consists of some 2,000 low-lying coral islands, only about 215 of which are inhabited, grouped in twelve clearly-defined clusters or atolls but divided for administrative purposes into eighteen atolls. The islands are small, and Male the capital is only a mile long and half-a-mile across. The population of about 90,000 is of mixed race of probably Aryan original stock speaking a language akin to Elu or old Sinhalese. The climate is very warm and humid.

The islands are covered with coconut palms and yield millet and fruit as well as coconut produce. The principal industry is fishing, and 'Maldive fish', prepared from the bonito, is the main export. Other occupations are coir and lace making. Communication for the most part is with Ceylon, India and Pakistan.

Until 31st December 1952 the islands were a Sultanate under the protection of Her Majesty. The Sultanate was elective. As from the 1st January 1953 a Republic was inaugurated, but in February 1954 it was decided to revert to Sultanate Government. His Highness Al Amir Mohamed Farid Didi was installed as Sultan on the 7th March 1954. The Prime Minister is the Hon. Ibrahim Nasir. There is a Parliament (the Majlis) with representatives elected from all the atolls.

Relations with Britain. The Islands have long enjoyed the protection of the British Crown, and this was formally recorded in an exchange of letters between the Sultan and the Governor of Ceylon in December 1887. On the independence of Ceylon in 1948, a new agreement dated the 24th April 1948 was signed between the Sultan and the British Government. This provided that the Islands should remain under the protection of the Crown, that their external affairs should be conducted by, or in accordance with the advice of, the British Government, that the British Government should refrain from any interference in the internal affairs of the Islands, and that the Sultan should afford such facilities for H.M. Forces as the British Government might, after consultation, consider necessary for the defence of the Islands or of the Commonwealth. Relations between the Maldive Islands and Britain were to be conducted through the British High Commissioner in Ceylon. These provisions were reaffirmed in a new agreement of the 1st January 1953.

Following negotiations with the Maldivian Government, a revised agreement was signed on the 14th February 1960 making certain changes in the agreement of 1953, particularly with regard to the conduct by the Maldivian Government of relations with other Governments in certain fields, and providing for the establishment of a British airfield on Gan Island in Addu Atoll. At the same time, by an exchange of letters, the British Government undertook to make available to the Maldivian Government substantial financial aid.

CHAPTER 42
BRUNEI

THE State of Brunei is situated on the north-west coast of Borneo and lies between latitudes $4^\circ 2'$ and $5^\circ 3'$ North and longitudes $114^\circ 4'$ and $115^\circ 22'$ East. It has an area of 2,226 square miles, and has a coast line of approximately one hundred miles. It forms an enclave between Sarawak on the west and south and Sabah on the east. There is a narrow coastal plain intersected by several rivers which descend from the hilly hinterland.

The population at the 1960 census totalled 83,877, consisting in the main of Malays (54 per cent) and Chinese (26 per cent). The climate is tropical and is characterised by a uniform temperature throughout the year, a high humidity and heavy rainfall, varying from 100 inches a year at the coast to over 200 inches in certain parts of the interior. The principal towns are Brunei Town, the capital (population 17,000), Seria and Kuala Belait. Brunei Town and Kuala Belait are the two ports.
The main crops are padi, rubber and sago; but the main product is crude oil from the Seria field, which produced 4,511,000 tons in 1960. Principal exports are crude oil, rubber and natural gas.

Brunei is a Sultanate, the present Ruler, the twenty-eighth of his line, being His Highness Sir Omar Ali Saifuddin Wasa'dul Khairi Waddin, DK, PSNB, SPMB, DMN, DK (Kelantan), DK (Johore), DK (Selangor), KCMG, who succeeded to the throne on 6th June 1950. On the 29th September 1959 a new constitution was promulgated, providing for a Privy Council, an Executive Council and a Legislative Council, the latter replacing the Brunei State Council. A Chief Minister (Mentri Besar) is appointed by the Sultan and is responsible to him for the exercise of executive authority. The Chief Minister is Dato Marsal bin Maun, DSNB, SMB, POAS.

Relations with Britain. In 1847 the Sultan entered into a Treaty with Britain for the furtherance of commercial relations and for the suppression of piracy. By a further Treaty in 1888 Brunei was placed under British protection, and the Sultan agreed that the foreign relations of the State should be conducted by Her Majesty's Government. In 1905 a supplementary Agreement was entered into whereby the Sultan undertook to accept a British officer, to be styled a Resident, who should be the agent and representative of the British Government under the High Commissioner for the Malay States. The Governor of Sarawak was High Commissioner for Brunei from 1948 until 1959.

A new Agreement was concluded on 29th September 1959 whereby Britain continued to be responsible for the defence and external affairs of the State, and a separate post of High Commissioner for Brunei was created. In December 1962 an armed revolt against the government was overcome with British assistance.
The Sultan has been informed that the inclusion of Brunei in Malaysia would be welcomed at any time should Brunei wish to join.

It was expected that responsibility for British relations with the State of Brunei would be transferred from the Colonial Secretary to the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations during November 1963.

CHAPTER 43 FEDERATION OF RHODESIA AND NYASALAND

IN 1951 a London conference of officials of the governments of Northern Rhodesia, Southern Rhodesia and Nyasaland, and of the British Government, recommended in their report that there was an urgent need of some form of closer political association between the three Central African territories. The report recommended that this association should take the form of a federation, under which the Federal Government should have specified powers to deal mainly with the problems common to all three territories, leaving the residual matters with the territorial governments.

During the course of the next two years these proposals were examined at a series of Ministerial Conferences in London and Africa. The Conference of Ministers which met in London in January 1953 produced a final draft of the Federal Constitution. This was approved by the Legislative Councils of Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland and by a referendum in Southern Rhodesia. The Rhodesia and Nyasaland Federation Act 1953 (1 & 2 Eliz. 2. c. 30) providing for the federation of Southern Rhodesia, Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland by Order in Council received the Royal Assent on the 14th July 1953. Following an Order in Council (S.I. 1953 No. 1199) setting out the Constitution, the Federation came into being on the 3rd September 1953.

A provision in the Constitution required that the Constitution should be reviewed between seven and nine years after the date on which it came into force. A Commission under the chairmanship of the Viscount Monckton of Brenchley was appointed in 1959 to advise the Governments of Britain, the Federation and the three Territories in preparation for this review. The Report of the Commission was published in October 1960 and recommended 'prompt and far-reaching reforms' in the Federal structure to remove 'existing sources of opposition and mistrust'. A Constitutional Review Conference, consisting of delegations chosen by the five Governments, met in London on 5th December 1960. The Conference adjourned on 17th December 1960 without reaching any decisions.

In August 1961 African Ministers appointed to the Nyasaland Government as the result of a new Constitution reaffirmed their electoral pledge to take Nyasaland out of the Federation. In December 1962 African Ministers also took office for the first time in Northern Rhodesia and expressed themselves as equally opposed to the continuation of the Federation in its existing form. The First Secretary of State, Mr. R. A. Butler, following his assumption of responsibility for Central African affairs in March 1962, himself paid two visits to the Federation and also appointed in June 1962 a team of advisers led by Sir Roger Stevens, Deputy Under-Secretary of State at the Foreign Office, to investigate the situation there. This team reported to him later that year.

However, in the face of growing opposition in the two northern Protectorates to the continuation of Federation, the British Government felt obliged to accept, on 19th December 1962 in the case of Nyasaland, and on 1st April 1963 in the
Digitized by Google
Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland
PART III

case of Southern and Northern Rhodesia, that these territories could not be kept in the Federation against their will, and therefore accepted the principle that any territory which so wished must be allowed to secede. The British Government became convinced, after consultations with the Governments concerned, that this decision was essential before further progress could be made towards the evolution of an effective relationship between the Central African territories which was acceptable to each of them.

A Conference was, in consequence, held at the Victoria Falls from 28th June to 3rd July 1963 to arrange for the orderly dissolution of the Federation in which the British, Federal, Southern Rhodesia and Northern Rhodesia governments took part. The Nyasaland government was represented by observers. There was full agreement that arrangements should be made for the orderly and speedy transfer of Federal responsibilities to the territories and that machinery should be set up to deal with the consequential problems involved and to work out a timetable for the processes of dissolution. At the time of going to press it was expected that the Federation would come to an end formally on 31st December 1963.
A detailed history of the setting up of the Federation was included in Chapter 34 of the 1963 edition of The Commonwealth Relations Office List.

CHAPTER 44
EAST AFRICAN COMMON SERVICES ORGANIZATION
EAST AFRICAN GOVERNORS' CONFERENCE

FOLLOWING a recommendation of a Parliamentary Commission which visited East Africa in 1924 a Conference of Governors of the British East African territories was held in 1926, to discuss matters of mutual concern. It was decided that a permanent Conference Secretariat should be established at Nairobi and that Conferences should be held when necessary. Subsequently the Joint Select Committee on Closer Union in East Africa recommended that the machinery of the Governors' Conference should be increasingly used for ensuring continuous and effective co-operation and co-ordination in all matters of common interest to the East African territories. The Conference was placed in permanent session, to be convened whenever required, and it was decided that there should be annual meetings of the Governors of Kenya, Tanganyika and Uganda, attended, if desired, by the Governors of Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland and by the British Resident, Zanzibar.
EAST AFRICA HIGH COMMISSION

The East Africa High Commission replaced the East African Governors' Conference on 1st January 1948. The High Commission, consisting of the Governors of Kenya, Tanganyika and Uganda, was charged with the administration of certain services common to the three territories, e.g. The East African Directorate of Civil Aviation, The East African Posts and Telegraphs Department, the East African Meteorological Department, etc. The East Africa Central Legislative Assembly was established in 1947, and in 1956 its membership was increased from 24 to 34, consisting of a Speaker, 7 ex officio members, 6 nominated members and 20 unofficial members. Two nominated members were appointed by each of the Governors of Kenya, Tanganyika and Uganda. Six unofficial members were returned from each territory, of whom three were appointed in each case by the Governor, and the other three elected. In addition two Arab unofficial members were appointed by the High Commission. The High Commission had power to legislate, with the advice and consent of the Assembly, in respect of inter-territorial common services, and on any matter concerned with the peace, order and good government of the Territories. The establishment of the High Commission involved no change in the constitution or administrative responsibilities of the Governments of the three territories, which remained responsible for basic services such as administration, police, health, education, agriculture, forestry, labour, housing and public works.
EAST AFRICAN COMMON SERVICES ORGANIZATION
At the Constitutional Conference in Dar es Salaam in March 1961 the Tanganyika Government expressed the wish to continue participation after
Digitized by Google
East African Common Services Organization
PART III

independence, in the common services provided by the East Africa High Commission, in a manner compatible with Tanganyika's independence. Arrangements to this end were worked out in talks, held in London in June 1961 and attended by delegates from Britain, Tanganyika, Kenya and Uganda, and of the East Africa High Commission, and by an observer from Zanzibar. It was agreed that, in the interests of all the Territories concerned, common services should continue to be provided on an East African basis, and that this should be secured, when Tanganyika became independent, by setting up a new organization called the East African Common Services Organization. Tanganyika, Kenya and Uganda would participate as equal partners, responsibility for the policy of the new Organization being vested in a group consisting of the principal elected Ministers of the three Territories. This group, called the East African Common Services Authority, is supported by four groups, each composed of a 'triumvirate' of Territorial Ministers, and each responsible for a specified field of subjects, viz. Communications, Finance, Commercial and Industrial Co-ordination and Social and Research Services. 'Parliamentary control' is exercised by a Central Legislative Assembly consisting of the twelve Ministers comprising the four triumvirates, nine members elected by each Territorial Legislature, and the two principal executive officers of the Organization, the Secretary-General and the Legal Secretary. The Assembly is empowered to pass measures relating to the matters for which the Organization is responsible, measures which would, when duly assented to, have the force of law in each Territory. These arrangements (which are fully described in Cmnd. 1433) were brought into effect on the 9th December 1961 by an Agreement between the Government of independent Tanganyika and the Governments of Kenya and Uganda, to whom the necessary powers were entrusted by the British Government.
The services of the Organization are extended to Zanzibar on an agency basis on the request of the Zanzibar Government.

In June 1963 the Prime Ministers of Tanganyika, Uganda and Kenya met in Nairobi and issued a Declaration stating that they believed that a political federation of East Africa was desired by their peoples and that they were convinced that the time had come to create a Federation of the three countries. With this in view a Working Party was set up to prepare a draft constitution for a Federation of East Africa for subsequent consideration by Governments. In July 1963 Zanzibar associated itself with the July declaration.
The notes on the East African Common Services Organization which are given below describe the Organization as it existed at the time of going to press, which was before Kenya attained independence.
The principal functions of the East African Common Services Organization are:—
(a) to administer the following services:—
The East African Railways and Harbours Administration
The East African Posts and Telecommunications Administration
The East African Directorate of Civil Aviation
The East African Meteorological Department
The East African Customs and Excise Department
The East African Income Tax Department
The East African Research Services
The East Africa Office in London

Responsibility for the Organization's policy rests with the East African Common Services Authority, i.e. the principal elected ministers of Tanganyika, Kenya and Uganda, which has the capacity of a body corporate with perpetual succession. The Authority is supported by four groups, each composed of three ministers (one from each territory) who are responsible for formulating policy in the specified fields of communications, finance, commercial and industrial co-ordination and social and research services.

The Organization's Central Legislative Assembly comprises the twelve ministers making up the four groups supporting the Authority, nine members from each territory elected by the territorial legislature and the Secretary-General

The enactment of measures of the Organization is effected by means of bills passed by the Central Legislative Assembly and assented to on behalf of the Organization by the President of Tanganyika, the Governor of Kenya and the President of Uganda on the recommendation of the East African Common Services Authority. Until the attainment of independence by Kenya the Governor of Kenya could be instructed by the British Government to withhold assent to any of the Organization's measures which impinged on the defence and internal security of Kenya or which might conflict with the international obligations of the British Government.

The establishment of the East African Common Services Organization has involved no change in the constitution or administrative responsibilities of the Governments of the three countries, which remain responsible for the basic

CHAPTER 44 East African Common Services Organization
services such as administration, police, health, education, agriculture, animal health, forestry, labour, housing and public works.

The East African Railways and Harbours Administration and the East African Posts and Telecommunications Administration are self-contained and self-financing services. The other services (non-self-contained) administered by the Organization are financed from a Distributable Pool under the Organization's control, which is derived from a proportion of the customs and excise revenue collected in the three territories and a proportion of the income tax on the profits of certain types of companies.
COURT OF APPEAL

The East African Common Services Organization Agreement has been modified to provide for the constitution of a Court of Appeal for Eastern Africa to be a successor court to Her Majesty's Court of Appeal for Eastern Africa (as constituted by the Eastern Africa Court of Appeal Order in Council 1961). A supplementary Agreement for that purpose was signed on behalf of the Governments of Tanganyika, Uganda and Kenya and came into effect from the 28th November 1962.
RESEARCH SERVICES
Research Services administered by the Organization at present are:—

The East African Agriculture and Forestry Research Organization, incorporating the former East African Agricultural Research Institute at Amani in Tanganyika and including the East African Herbarium, Nairobi; and attached temporary schemes. The organization is responsible for research on certain aspects of animal industry in addition to agriculture and forestry. The headquarters are at Muguga, within easy reach of Nairobi and are shared with the East African Veterinary Research Organization.

The East African Veterinary Research Organization which is responsible for research on animal diseases and other aspects of animal industry and for the production of vaccines for certain of these diseases.
The East African Trypanosomiasis Research Organization which is responsible for entomological and trypanosomiasis research, animal and human; the headquarters are at Tororo in Uganda.
The East African Freshwater Fisheries Research Organization which is responsible for the fisheries of inland waters; with headquarters at Jinja in Uganda.
The East African Marine Fisheries Research Organization which is responsible for marine fisheries; with headquarters at Zanzibar.
The East African Industrial Research Organization which has its headquarters and main laboratory in Nairobi. A Board is responsible for policy with regard to industrial research.

The Medical Research Division which includes the East African Leprosy Research Centre at Itesio in Kenya, the Virus Research Institute at Entebbe in Uganda, the Institute for Medical Research at Mwanza in Tanganyika and the Institute of Malaria and Vector-borne Diseases at Amani in Tanganyika.
The first four Organizations shown above are under the general control of the East African Agricultural and Fisheries Research Council which was established in 1954.

SCIENTIFIC SERVICES
Scientific Services administered by the Organization are:—

The East African Statistical Department, situated at Nairobi, which is responsible for East African statistics on trade and transport, finance generally, balance of payments, fuels, insurance and the presentation of statistics in respect of the East African Common Market.

The East African Meteorological Department which has its headquarters and central forecast organization at Nairobi and maintains regional headquarters at Dar es Salaam and Entebbe. It is responsible for the maintenance and study of meteorological records in all parts of East Africa and for weather forecasting, for aviation and other uses.

On the 7th April 1963 the Australian Government announced that a decimal currency is to be introduced in Australia. The tentative date for the changeover is February 1966. The major unit will be known as a Dollar and will be equal to the present 10 shillings (Australian). The minor unit will be known as a Cent and will be equal to one hundredth part of the major unit. No decision has yet been made on the full range of banknotes but there will be six coinage units; 50 cents, 20 cents, 10 cents 5 cents and 1 cent.

The New Zealand Government also announced, on 10th April 1963, that New Zealand would change to a decimal currency in 1967. The major unit will be equal to the present 10 shillings (New Zealand) and the minor unit would be one hundredth part of the major unit.
The Sierra Leone Government has announced that it is intended to introduce a 10-shilling unit to be known as the 'Leone' in the latter half of 1964. The 'Leone' will be divided into 100 cents.

CHAPTER 48
PUBLIC HOLIDAYS IN THE COMMONWEALTH

THE following table of statutory public holidays to be observed during 1964 has been prepared from the information available at the time of going to press but some countries were not due to publish their final lists until the end of 1963. In Asia and Africa some of the religious holidays are subject to the appearance of the moon and precise dates are decided by religious leaders shortly before the holidays occur. Such holidays are indicated in the table by the symbol *.

In certain Commonwealth countries additional public holidays are observed in provincial territories. For example, apart from the statutory public holidays observed throughout the Commonwealth of Australia each State observes its own additional holidays and these are shown separately in the table. In Cyprus the Greek and Turkish communities each observe their own holidays in addition to the four national holidays of the Republic and these are included in the table. A list of public holidays observed in the individual States of Malaysia was not available at the time of going to press. Federal public holidays included in the table which fall on a weekly holiday in any State of Malaysia are observed by the States concerned on the following day and if such latter day is already a holiday then the next day is observed as a holiday.
The Offices of British High Commissioners in Commonwealth capitals will be closed for public business during 1964 on statutory holidays observed in the capitals except as stated below.

India. Not closed on Ram Naumi, Buddha Purnima, Id-ul-Zuha and Guru Nanak's Birthday. The Office will be closed on other holidays shown in the table and on New Year's Day, 30th March (Easter Monday), 13th June (The Queen's Birthday) and Boxing Day.

Ceylon. The Office will be closed on all statutory holidays observed in Britain and certain Ceylonese holidays to a total of twelve days in 1964. The Ceylonese holidays were due to be chosen towards the end of 1963 after the final list of holidays had been decided by the Government of Ceylon.

Cyprus. The Office will be closed on the four national holidays and on 6th January (Epiphany), one day of Ramazan Bairam, 27th March (Good Friday), 28th March (Easter Saturday), one day of Qurban Bairam, 1st, 2nd and 4th May (Greek Orthodox Easter), one day in July (Birthday of the Prophet), 28th October (Ohi Day) and 25th/26th December.
Sierra Leone. The Office will be closed in addition on 13th June (The Queen's Birthday).
Jamaica. Not closed on 12th February (Ash Wednesday).
The British Embassy, Dublin, will be closed on all public holidays observed in Britain and on 17th March (St. Patrick's Day).

ABBey, Donald Alfred; born 7th April 1922; educated Ruckholt School, Leyton; m. 1945 Olive Pinwell (1 s. 1948; 1 d. 1950); enlisted Royal Army Ordnance Corps 1941–46; Temporary Clerk, India Office, 7th January 1947; Executive Officer 3rd September 1955; British Information Services, Johannesburg, 1956–59; Passport Officer, Karachi, 1960–62; Higher Executive Officer 1st September 1963.

I; m. 1952 Diana Synnott (1 s. 1956); Indian Civil Service 16th September 1937 to 1947, including three years in Army as Assistant and later Deputy Recruiting Officer under Lucknow Recruiting Area; commissioned 2nd Lieutenant April 1941 and placed in charge of recruitment for Province of Bihar and some of the Eastern States; reverted to Government of Bihar with rank of temporary Major February 1944; District Magistrate and Deputy Commissioner 1944 to 1947 with short period as Additional Director of Agriculture; Temporary Principal, Treasury, 17th July 1947; Commonwealth Relations Office 1st October 1947; Principal 28th May 1948; Dacca July 1947 to October 1950; Colombo September 1952 to March 1956; Deputy High Commissioner, Dacca (local Assistant Secretary), August 1960; Assistant Secretary 1st May 1962.

YEOMAN, Kenneth Stanley; born 9th August 1920; educated Cardinal Vaughan School, London, and Mayfield College, Sussex; m. 1948 Ursula Feord (3 d. 1949, 1951, 1958); Clerical Officer, G.P.O., 21st July 1937; mobilised R.N.V.R. 27th September 1939; commissioned Sub-Lieutenant, R.N.V.R., 1943; demobilised 1946; Executive Officer, G.P.O., 1st November 1947; on temporary transfer Malaya 1952–56; on contract, Nigeria, 1957–61; Higher Executive Officer, G.P.O., (in absentia ) 1st April 1960; Commonwealth Relations Office 16th June 1961; Karachi March 1962; Lahore May 1962.

YOUNG, Eric; born 16th November 1924; educated Maltby Grammar School, Yorkshire, 1935–42 and Sheffield University 1942–43 and 1946–48; Honours Class II Division I in English Language and Literature 1948; B.A.; m. 1949 Sheila Hutchinson (1 d. 1956; 2 s. 1958, 1961); Royal Navy 1943–46; served in Mediterranean, Indian Ocean and Far East; joined Sheffield Telegraph as reporter and leader-writer July 1948; sub-editor, Western Morning News , Plymouth, 1951; sub-editor and later regional news editor, Guardian , Manchester, 1953–58; visited West Africa as reporter 1958; Public Relations Officer, National Coal Board, Manchester, 1958–59; Temporary Senior Information Officer, Colonial Office, and Deputy Director, United Kingdom Information Office in Tanganyika and Zanzibar, January 1960; Senior Information Officer March 1961; Commonwealth Relations Office as Deputy Director, British Information Services, Tanganyika, 19th December 1961 to September 1963; Regional Information Officer (local Principal Information Officer), Kaduna, November 1963.

YOUNG, Joffre Alfred; born 26th March 1915; educated Merton School; m. 1941 Ethel Violet Welfare; Post Office 1929; Clerical Officer, Savings Bank Department, 1938; Clerical Officer, Canterbury Telephone Area, 1948; Higher Clerical Officer, Shrewsbury Telephone Area, 1950; Higher Executive Officer, (Treasury Pooling Scheme), Commonwealth Relations Office, 11th September 1961; Dacca October 1962.

Excellent reading for the student of current affairs, this series has been widely acclaimed by both critics and public alike. Written by specialists, each volume provides a contemporary picture of one of the United Kingdom's dependent territories. Authoritative and readable, vivid yet accurate, they fill the gap between the official report and the travelogue of the occasional visitor. Fully illustrated with photographs, maps and line drawings.

25th May, 1888. TENDERS. RANGE FINDERS FOR INFANTRY, FOR H.M. SERVICE. HIS ROYAL HIGHNESS the COMMANDER-IN-CHIEF is prepared to receive Proposals from Persons desirous of submitting for competitive trial RANGE FINDERS, which shall fulfil the following conditions, viz. :— (1). The instrument must be strong and simple and must not require frequent adjustment, nor extra- ordinary precautions against the exigencies of Field Service and effects of climate and weather. (2). It must be possible for one Infantry Soldier to carry the entire apparatus, in addition to his rifle and ordinary equipment, both on the march and in action. (3). A person of ordinary intelligence and normal vision must be able to become an efficient range taker after a month's training. (4). The system must be suitable for the observation of moving objects, especially troops of all arms in the usual formations up to 1200 yards distance. (5). It must be practicable to make at least four observations per minute with an average error, at 1000 yards, not exceeding 4 per cent. of the range. (6). The range must be read in yards without recourse to calculation. (7). The number of observers required for each instrument or set of instruments must not exceed two. (8). Other considerations being equal, preference will be given to those
on seeing this invitation to tender in 1888 successfully submitted a rangefinder of revolutionary design which proved to be the forerunner of all modern precision rangefinders.
